data.title: Pnc expects 2026 net interest income up 14.5% following FirstBank deal and 11% average loan growth outlook
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: The PNC Financial Services Group (PNC) Q1 2026

Management View

  • “We completed the acquisition of FirstBank early in the quarter, and we're well on our way to a mid-June conversion,” said “Organic loan growth hit a 3-year high, net interest margin expanded meaningfully, and we had 13% year-over-year fee income growth” (Chairman & CEO William Demchak).
  • “We are not seeing anything that suggests these issues are broadly impacting our customers or our credit quality in the near term,” Demchak said, adding on nondepository financial institution exposure: “we don't see any loss content in this book and certainly don't see any exposure to a systemic event.” (Chairman & CEO Demchak).
  • “As a result, our overall balance sheet growth includes the impact of the acquisition, which represented $15 billion in loans and $22 billion in deposits,” and “during the quarter, we returned $1.4 billion of capital to shareholders,” including “approximately $700 million each” in dividends and buybacks (Executive VP & CFO Robert Reilly).

Outlook

  • “Our outlook for the second quarter of 2026 compared to the first quarter of 2026 is as follows: we expect average loans to be up 2% to 3%, net interest income to be up approximately 3%, fee income to be up 2.5%, other noninterest income to be in the range of $150 million to $200 million,” and “we expect total revenue to be up approximately 3.5%” (Executive VP & CFO Reilly).
  • “Our outlook for the full year 2026 compared to 2025 results is as follows: we expect full year average loan growth to be up approximately 11%,” “full year net interest income to be up approximately 14.5%,” “noninterest income to be up approximately 6%,” and “total revenue to be up approximately 11%” (Executive VP & CFO Reilly).
  • Change vs. prior-quarter framing: in Q4 2025, Reilly said, “We expect the Fed to cut rates 2x in 2026,” while in Q1 2026, Reilly updated, “We do not expect the Federal Reserve to cut rates during 2026” (Executive VP & CFO Reilly).

Financial Results

  • “For the linked quarter, loans of $351 billion grew by $23 billion or 7%,” “deposit balances were up $19 billion or 4% and averaged $458 billion,” and “we remain well capitalized with an estimated CET1 ratio of 10.1%, down 50 basis points from year-end 2025” (Executive VP & CFO Reilly).
  • “Total revenue was $6.2 billion,” “net interest income of $4 billion increased $230 million or 6%,” and “our net interest margin was 2.95%, an increase of 11 basis points” (Executive VP & CFO Reilly).
  • Profitability and items impacting comparability: “our first quarter net income was $1.8 billion or $4.13 per common share and $4.32 when adjusted for integration costs,” while “noninterest expense of $3.8 billion increased $165 million or 5%, of which $97 million was integration expense” (Executive VP & CFO Reilly).
  • Credit and private credit/NDFI detail: “nonperforming loans… represented 0.62% of total loans,” “our allowance for credit losses totaled $5.5 billion or 1.52% of total loans,” and Reilly said the business credit intermediaries portion includes “approximately $7 billion” mostly CLOs, concluding, “expect zero losses going forward” (Executive VP & CFO Reilly).

Q&A

  • Ebrahim Poonawala, BofA Securities: asked about growing core deposits in a higher-for-longer rate environment; Chairman & CEO Demchak responded, “our focus has been on growing retail clients,” adding, “We’ve opened [8] so far this year,” and “Our digital acquisition has been really strong.”
  • Robert Siefers, Piper Sandler: pressed on conservatism in loan-growth outlook; Executive VP & CFO Reilly said, “we do have some paydowns that are coming,” and Chairman & CEO Demchak added, “We can't predict it, but we banked some in the first quarter.”
  • Manan Gosalia, Morgan Stanley: asked why higher loan-growth outlook doesn’t translate proportionally to NII; Reilly said, “it's loan mix,” with “a much higher volume of higher credit quality deals… carry relatively lower spreads,” while reiterating, “We still expect to go above 3% in the second half.”
  • John Pancari, Evercore ISI: asked on capital markets pipelines and M&A; Reilly said, “their pipelines are strong,” and “for the full year, still up double digits” (re Harris Williams) (Executive VP & CFO Reilly).
  • Kenneth Usdin, Bernstein Autonomous: asked on FirstBank conversion timing and expense cadence; Reilly said, “We are still planning to convert mid-June,” with “nonrecurring merger and integration costs… approximately $325 million,” including “approximately $150 million in the second quarter” (Executive VP & CFO Reilly).
  • Christopher McGratty, KBW: challenged private credit loss concerns; Demchak responded, “It's not even on the curve,” and “this isn't even on the page of what we're looking at.”
  • Matthew O'Connor, Deutsche Bank: asked about rate positioning and MSR hedges; Demchak said, “We are basically economic value of capital flat,” and on MSR, “we got chopped up… realized vol was way higher than implied.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst sentiment: slightly negative to pressing, with repeated questions on deposit competition, loan-growth durability, private credit/NDFI exposure, and Basel capital usage.
  • Management sentiment: slightly positive in prepared remarks (“we're off to a really strong start this year”) and more emphatic in Q&A on NDFI (“expect zero losses going forward”) while acknowledging volatility impacts (“we got chopped up”).
  • Versus last quarter: tone stayed constructive, but Q1 2026 included more direct rebuttal of market worries around private credit and a clearer rates stance (“We do not expect the Federal Reserve to cut rates during 2026”) versus Q4 2025’s projected cuts.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Strategy continuity with added emphasis: both quarters highlighted FirstBank integration and branch/technology investment, while Q1 2026 put more weight on defending NDFI/private credit exposure and detailing composition (trade receivable securitizations vs. CLOs).
  • Guidance language shifted on rates: Q4 2025 guided “cut rates 2x in 2026,” while Q1 2026 stated no cuts expected; management also reiterated buyback pace expectations (“range of $600 million to $700 million going forward”).
  • Key operating momentum discussed in Q1 2026 included “net interest margin… 2.95%” and CRE “reached an inflection point,” reinforcing prior-quarter commentary that CRE had “largely stabilized” (Executive VP & CFO Reilly).

Risks and Concerns

  • “Many market concerns out there from energy prices to AI to private credit,” but management said, “we are not seeing anything” broadly affecting customers near term (Chairman & CEO Demchak).
  • Deposit competition was acknowledged: Demchak noted “people are paying up to maintain balances,” while asserting PNC “don't have to go and lead with our faces here on price.”
  • Earnings sensitivity to volatility showed up in mortgage/MSR: “Mortgage revenue decreased… largely attributable to… heightened rate volatility,” and Demchak said MSR hedging was hurt as “realized vol was way higher than implied” (Executive VP & CFO Reilly; Chairman & CEO Demchak).

Final Takeaway

PNC’s management characterized Q1 2026 as a “really strong start,” anchored by FirstBank’s addition and legacy C&I growth, alongside an updated rates view that assumes no Fed cuts in 2026. Reilly’s outlook called for Q2 revenue up about 3.5% and full-year 2026 net interest income up about 14.5%, while management repeatedly pushed back on investor concerns about private credit exposure, emphasizing the investment-grade and collateralized nature of the NDFI book and stating they “expect zero losses going forward.”

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: BP raised to Buy at UBS as new CEO takes over 'at critical turning pont'
data.description:
BP (BP) is little changed in Wednesday's trading as UBS upgraded shares to Buy from Neutral, as CEO Meg O'Neill takes over at "a critical turning point for the company," with work to do to regain investor confidence and reduce the stock's 50%-plus underperformance compared to peers since 2018.

UBS analyst Joshua Stone identified four potential areas of focus ahead of an expected strategy update later in the year, including right-sizing the company's cost base, noting BP (BP) has the highest cost intensity of peers, with total operating expenses up ~$10B since 2019.

Stone's analysis suggests $3B-$6B in potential savings from current levels, which represents a 20% increase to pre-tax earnings at the midpoint.

BP (BP) also has the highest leverage ratio in its sector at 47%, and higher prices help to speed up deleveraging, with Stone saying he now sees the ratio falling to 27% by 2028 in a base case scenario; the analyst thinks capex can be held at $13B-$14B until 2027, but may need to increase to ~$15B from 2028 to fund new projects.

data.title: Bessent sees oil prices falling to around $3/gallon this summer
data.description:
U.S. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said he sees oil prices falling to around $3 per gallon over the summer.

“I’m optimistic that sometime between June 20 and September 20, we can have $3 gas again,” Bessent told reporters at the White House on Wednesday, according to Bloomberg.

Bessent added that officials from several Middle Eastern countries have said that once the Strait of Hormuz is reopened, they can begin pumping oil within one week. 

Bloomberg noted that the average price of a gallon of gas this week was $4.108, citing the American Automobile Association.

Bessent also said the government would be monitoring gas stations to make sure their prices are in line with the price of oil.

“We are going to be watching the gas stations, because they raised prices very quickly,” Bessent said, adding, “We hope they’ll bring them down just as quickly as crude oil prices have come down, which they’ve come down substantially, just in the past 10 days.”

data.title: Top mid to large cap U.S. homebuilding stocks by Quant rating as builder sentiment falls
data.description:
The U.S. homebuilding sector is flashing fresh warning signs heading into Q1 2026 earnings season, as builder confidence tumbled four points to 34 in April, the lowest reading since September 2025 and well below the consensus estimate of 37, driven by elevated mortgage rates, surging material costs tied to higher fuel prices, and deepening economic uncertainty.

Against this challenging backdrop, below is a list of U.S. mid to large-cap homebuilding stocks ranked according to their Seeking Alpha Quant Ratings.

Champion Homes, Inc. (SKY) tops the list with the only Buy rating at 3.74. Tri Pointe Homes, Inc. (TPH) and Installed Building Products, Inc. (IBP) follow with Hold ratings of 3.13 and 3.01, respectively.

The list spans major industry players including Toll Brothers (TOL), PulteGroup (PHM), and D.R. Horton (DHI), all carrying Hold ratings. At the bottom, TopBuild Corp. (BLD) and Cavco Industries (CVCO) hold Sell ratings of 1.89 and 1.74, indicating bearish assessments from the Quant system.

Seeking Alpha’s Quant Ratings grade stocks on their relative performance across key quantitative measures, including valuation, growth, stock momentum, and profitability. Ratings are given on a scale from 1 to 5, with any rating of 3.5 or above considered bullish and any rating of 2.5 or below considered bearish.

Here is the list:

  1. Champion Homes, Inc. (SKY), Quant Rating: 3.74

  2. Tri Pointe Homes, Inc. (TPH), Quant Rating: 3.13

  3. Installed Building Products, Inc. (IBP), Quant Rating: 3.01

  4. Toll Brothers, Inc. (TOL), Quant Rating: 2.90

  5. M/I Homes, Inc. (MHO), Quant Rating: 2.81

  6. Taylor Morrison Home Corporation (TMHC), Quant Rating: 2.79

  7. NVR, Inc. (NVR), Quant Rating: 2.79

  8. PulteGroup, Inc. (PHM), Quant Rating: 2.75

  9. Green Brick Partners, Inc. (GRBK), Quant Rating: 2.74

  10. D.R. Horton, Inc. (DHI), Quant Rating: 2.72

  11. Lennar Corporation (LEN), Quant Rating: 2.71

  12. Meritage Homes Corporation (MTH), Quant Rating: 2.65

  13. KB Home (KBH), Quant Rating: 2.64

  14. TopBuild Corp. (BLD), Quant Rating: 1.89

  15. Cavco Industries, Inc. (CVCO), Quant Rating: 1.74

data.title: FTC accuses ad giants of colluding to steer ad dollars from conservative platforms
data.description:
The Federal Trade Commission ((FTC)) in conjunction with several state attorneys general, filed a  lawsuit against advertising firms WPP Media (WPP), Publicis, Inc. (PGPEF), and Dentsu US (DNTUF) (DNTUY), alleging collusion by jointly boycotting online media platforms based on political content that contained what the defendants considered “misinformation.” 

According to the complaint, in 2018 the defendants allegedly agreed to adopt the same “brand safety standards” on misinformation, establishing the Advertising Protection Bureau with the aim of putting competition aside to become one voice and steer clients’ ads away from media platforms ad agencies deemed politically conservative and, thus, ineligible for advertising revenue.

As a result, “conservative publishers identified as publishing what the ‘Brand Safety Floor’ defined as ‘misinformation’ suffered dramatic declines in their sales of digital advertising inventory,” the FTC complaint says.

“This unlawful collusion not only damaged our marketplace but also distorted the marketplace of ideas by discriminating against speech and ideas that fell below the unlawfully agreed-upon floor,” FTC Chairman Andrew Fuerguson said in a statement.

One of these online platforms mentioned in the complaint includes Fox News (FOX) (FOXA) and Elon Musk’s X.com.

In response, the ad agencies mentioned in the complaint have agreed to “stop unlawful collusion” and prevent similar conduct from occurring in the future, although did not admit to any wrongdoing.

Omnicom Group (OMC) was not included in the FTC lawsuit after agreeing, as part of its acquisition of Interpublic Group last year, not to steer ad dollars based on political content.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Cadence Design rises as it unveils Google Cloud, Nvidia deals
data.description:
Cadence Design Systems (CDNS) shares rose more than 4% on Wednesday after the electronic design automation company announced a deal with Google (GOOG) (GOOGL) and expanded its previous offering with Nvidia (NVDA).

Google Cloud

As part of the deal with Google, Cadence's ChipStack AI Super Agent will be optimized with Gemini on Google Cloud. It puts Cadence closer to the shift of agentic design automation, and can create an “agent-driven, scalable, cloud-native platform for next-generation chip design and verification,” the company said in a statement. 

“Our collaboration with Google Cloud represents a major step forward in scaling AI-driven design automation,” said Cadence executive Paul Cunningham in the release. “By integrating the Cadence ChipStack AI Super Agent with Gemini, we’re advancing the next generation of agentic design—combining the reasoning power of large language models with Cadence’s world-class EDA engines to deliver breakthrough productivity and quality of results for our customers.”

Nvidia and robots

Separately, on Wednesday, Cadence said it has expanded its deal with Nvidia to bring its systems and services to agentic AI, physics-based simulation, and digital twins. They are  also working on using artificial intelligence for robots, the CEOs of companies said.

“We are at an inflection point in computing—CUDA-accelerated computing and AI are reinventing the engineering process,” said Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang. “For the first time, we can innovate in the digital world—exploring, testing, and optimizing ideas at unprecedented speed and scale—by building everything as full-fidelity digital twins first. Together, NVIDIA and Cadence are bringing this vision to life—transforming how engineers design, build and operate the world.”

“Agentic AI and digital twins are reshaping the entire engineering landscape—from semiconductor design to planetary‑scale AI systems,” Cadence CEO and President Anirudh Devgan added. “Our expanded collaboration with NVIDIA accelerates the convergence of design and physical realization, connecting the Cadence AgentStack, Physical AI Stack, and AI factory digital twins with NVIDIA’s breakthroughs in accelerated computing to deliver unprecedented speed, accuracy and trust in simulation and system development.”

data.title: SolarEdge cut to Sell at Goldman Sachs as expectations appear too high
data.description:
SolarEdge Technologies (SEDG) down 12.7% in Wednesday's trading as Goldman Sachs downgraded to Sell from Neutral with a $31 price target, trimmed from $36, based on the bank's fundamental view that the company's core end markets are not growing nearly as fast as current consensus expectations would imply.

Goldman analyst Brian Lee said SolarEdge's (SEDG) sales and margin recovery is more than fully appreciated in the stock's valuation, which currently trades at the highest price-to-earnings multiple among solar equipment peers.

The stock's recent rally also suggests expectations for potential upside from a material improvement in European demand, stemming from rising energy prices following the outbreak of war in Iran, appear to be creating a higher bar of expectations, Lee said, instead seeing downside risk to consensus earnings estimates in 2026-27.

Separately, Bank of America analysts said they see a negative read-through for SolarEdge (SEDG) from the Chapter 11 bankruptcy filing by Freedom Forever, the largest U.S. residential solar installer by market share, with an estimated 8.5% national share and 25% share in California.

data.title: L3Harris to expand rocket motor output with $1.27B Virginia investment
data.description:
L3Harris Technologies (LHX) plans to invest $1.27 billion to increase solid rocket motor production at its Orange County, Virginia facility, alongside an announcement from Abigail Spanberger on Wednesday.

The move follows a separate $1 billion commitment from the U.S. government to support the company’s rocket motor operations, aimed at securing supply for missile systems including Tomahawk missile and Patriot missile system.

Funding will include a $1 billion convertible preferred investment in L3Harris’ Missile Solutions unit, which is expected to be spun off and listed as a standalone propulsion company in the second half of 2026. The company said it will build new facilities at the Virginia site to support multiple Department of Defense programs.

The expansion comes amid elevated global demand for munitions as countries replenish stockpiles, a trend that has supported growth across the defense sector. L3Harris (LHX) also manufactures rocket motors at facilities in Arkansas and Alabama.

data.title: Mark Mobius, pioneer of emerging markets investing, dies at 89
data.description:
Mark Mobius, the globe-trotting investor who helped bring emerging markets into the mainstream for global portfolios, has died at age 89.

Mobius spent more than three decades at Franklin Templeton Investments, where he championed investment opportunities across Asia, Africa, Latin America and Eastern Europe at a time when many markets were largely closed to foreign capital. Hired by John Templeton in 1987, he went on to build and lead the Templeton Emerging Markets Group, overseeing funds that consistently outperformed benchmarks.

Known for his hands-on approach, Mobius spent much of the year traveling to developing economies, visiting companies directly to identify opportunities. His strategy paid off during key moments, including buying into distressed markets during the Asian financial crisis and Russia’s 1998 selloff, as well as anticipating the post-2009 emerging markets rally.

After retiring from Franklin Templeton in 2018, he founded Mobius Capital Partners and remained active in global markets, later launching another venture in Dubai.

Widely regarded as a defining figure in emerging markets investing, Mobius helped transform the asset class from a niche strategy into a core allocation for institutional investors, combining on-the-ground research with a willingness to invest where others hesitated.
